did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture integrated with a MobileNet pre-trained model for feature extraction and classification of skin lesions. It utilizes the ISIC dataset to train the model for diagnosing nine classes of skin cancer (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) and reports results such as 99.06% training accuracy and 98.11% validation accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a custom CNN architecture integrated with MobileNet for skin lesion classification. It details the use of the ISIC dataset, describes the methodology including Gaussian filtering and data augmentation, and reports specific quantitative results such as 99.06% training accuracy and 98.11% validation accuracy. Although published in a conference proceedings (GCWOT), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Improved Skin Lesion Classification Using Gaussian Filtering and Mobile Net-based Convolutional Neural Networks
journal: 2024 Global Conference on Wireless and Optical Technologies (GCWOT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture integrated with a MobileNet pre-trained model to classify nine classes of skin lesions, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. The model was trained and evaluated using the ISIC dataset, which was augmented to address class imbalance, resulting in 2,357 training, 1,325 validation, and 1,325 testing images. The system utilized Gaussian filtering for feature extraction and the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001% to enhance learning efficiency. The proposed model achieved a training accuracy of 99.06% and a validation accuracy of 98.11%, with low training and validation losses of 0.14 and 0.15, respectively. These results demonstrate that the deep learning approach significantly improves the accuracy of skin cancer detection compared to previous methods, highlighting its potential for early diagnosis and improved patient outcomes.

Mentioned in the introduction as a common form of skin cancer alongside melanoma. The model distinguishes between melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancer in some contexts, but the specific experimental classes are the nine listed above.
The paper mentions discriminating between tumor cells that are benign and those that are malignant as part of the diagnostic goal, alongside the specific nine-class classification.

all_task_types.target: Nine distinct classes of skin lesions (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Vascular Lesion).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model performs 'nine separate classifications' of skin cancer types using the ISIC dataset. It reports metrics such as training accuracy (99.06%), validation accuracy (98.11%), precision, recall, and F1-Score for each of the nine discrete diagnostic categories. The task is multiclass classification of dermatoscopic images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (via Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: A large public dataset of dermatoscopic images contributed by the ISIC Archive, accessed via Kaggle. It contains images illustrating various phases of skin cancer.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN integrated with MobileNet after image augmentation to address class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reports performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-Score, and compares these results qualitatively to other methods without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset.notes: The proposed model achieved 95.98% accuracy on the test data, outperforming the comparative baselines listed in Table 3. The model utilized Gaussian filtering for feature extraction and the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001%.

architecture_used.main_model: Custom CNN integrated with MobileNet (Mobile-Net) for feature extraction and classification
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNet pre-trained model used as foundation; Inception v3 layers were pre-trained and initially frozen (except first two layers) in comparative analysis, but the primary proposed model uses MobileNet

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Gaussian filtering and MobileNet-based feature extraction
custom_layers: Flatten layer (converts Inception v3/MobileNet output to 1D array)
Two dense layers with ReLU activation
Final dense layer with Softmax activation for 9 classes

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named, but training loss reported as 0.14 and validation loss as 0.15
training_details.augmentation: Image augmentation techniques applied to address class imbalance (scaling, geometry, intensity)

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian filtering for smoothing and noise removal
Image augmentation (scaling, geometry, intensity) to address class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to address class imbalance and enhance the volume of the dataset. Specific probabilities or ranges were not reported, but it resulted in increased image counts per class (e.g., Actinic Keratosis from 130 to 224, Melanoma from 454 to 887).
Used to handle class imbalance by punishing the loss of the majority class and combining extra photos with the minority classes.

conclusions: The proposed custom CNN architecture integrated with a MobileNet pre-trained model achieved a training accuracy of 99.06% and a validation accuracy of 98.11% on the ISIC dataset.
The model achieved a test accuracy of 95.98%, outperforming comparative baseline models including CNN (75%), Mobile Net + CNN (93.6%), CBAM (62.2%), and another CNN variant (93.1%).

The use of Gaussian filtering for feature extraction and the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001% contributed to low training (0.14) and validation (0.15) losses, indicating effective learning.

Image augmentation techniques were effective in addressing class imbalance, allowing the model to achieve high performance across nine distinct skin lesion classes, including Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Vascular Lesion.

The study highlights the potential of deep learning algorithms, specifically CNNs, to enhance the accuracy of skin cancer diagnoses, which can lead to improved patient outcomes and higher survival rates.

The model demonstrated high precision and recall for several classes, such as Basal Cell Carcinoma (Precision: 0.98, Recall: 1.0), Dermatofibroma (Precision: 1.0, Recall: 1.0), and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Precision: 1.0, Recall: 1.0), while showing lower performance for Actinic Keratosis (Recall: 0.55) and Seborrheic Keratosis (Precision: 0.49).
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC dataset accessed via Kaggle, which may introduce population bias and limits the generalizability of the results to other demographic groups or clinical settings.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) to validate the superiority of the proposed model over baselines, relying instead on point estimates of accuracy.

There is a notable performance disparity across classes; Actinic Keratosis shows low recall (0.55) and Seborrheic Keratosis shows low precision (0.49), indicating potential clinical risks for false negatives and false positives in these specific categories.
The model was trained and evaluated on a single split of the dataset without mention of external validation or cross-validation strategies, raising concerns about overfitting and robustness.
The hardware specifications used for evaluation (Intel Core i7-2720QM CPU, 32 GB RAM) are outdated, which may affect the reproducibility of training times and computational efficiency claims.

The paper claims a 'considerable increase' in patient outcomes and survival rates, which is a strong clinical assertion not supported by the experimental data, which only reports image classification metrics.

Data leakage risks are present due to the use of image augmentation (scaling, geometry, intensity) to address class imbalance; if augmentation parameters were tuned on the validation/test set, performance metrics may be optimistically biased.

The comparative baselines (CNN [28], Mobile Net + CNN [29], etc.) are cited from external references rather than re-implemented and re-trained on the exact same data split, making direct performance comparisons potentially invalid due to differing experimental conditions.
